lead: Moving day is the part everyone worries about, but it really does not have to be stressful. Over twenty years our crews have carried homes in and out of cottages, terraces and farmhouses right across Suffolk, and we've picked up the small habits that separate a smooth move from a frantic one. Here are ten genuinely useful moving day tips, drawn straight from the cab and the hallway, to help your day run calmly from the first knock on the door to the last box carried in.

sections.h: 1. Pack the Essentials Box and Keep It With You
sections.body:
If you take one tip from us, take this one. Before anything else gets boxed, pack a single, clearly marked essentials box and keep it in your own car rather than on the lorry. Into it goes the kettle, a few mugs, tea, milk and biscuits, phone chargers, basic toiletries, a change of clothes, any medication, important documents and a few toys if you have little ones. This is the box that keeps everyone going.

We've found that the families who do this stay noticeably calmer all day. When you arrive at the new place tired and surrounded by sealed boxes, being able to make a cup of tea and find a toothbrush without unpacking the whole van is worth its weight in gold. Treat that box as sacred; do not let it get loaded by mistake. Many a customer has spent the first night hunting for the kettle because it travelled to the wrong end of the county on the lorry.

If your move spans a couple of days, or there is a chance of a delay in the chain, pack the box a little fuller; add bin bags, kitchen roll, a torch, scissors and a small first aid kit. Keep it on the passenger seat where you can see it, and mark it boldly so nobody is tempted to be helpful and load it. It is the smallest job on the list and the one that pays you back the most.

sections.h: 2. Be Ready Before the Crew Arrives
sections.body:
The single biggest thing that slows a move down is packing that is not finished when we arrive. We turn up ready to load, so the more you have boxed, sealed and labelled the night before, the faster and cheaper your day will be. Aim to have everything packed except the kettle and the breakfast things, which can go in last. Dismantle any flat-pack furniture you are confident with, or leave it for us if you would rather.

It also helps enormously to have a plan for the morning. Strip the beds, empty the fridge and freezer the day before, and make sure nothing is still hidden in the loft or the shed. Open the curtains, switch on the lights so we can see, and clear a path from the front door to the kerb. A crew that can move freely from the off will have your home loaded far quicker than one tiptoeing around half-finished packing.

One thing we always say: do not leave the loft until the last minute. It is the room people forget, and discovering a dozen heavy boxes up a ladder when the lorry is half loaded throws the whole timetable out. Clear it the week before, and the same goes for the shed and garage, which hold far more than anyone expects. Get those out of the way and the actual moving day becomes a calm, steady process.

sections.h: 3. Label Boxes by Room, on the Side
sections.body:
Labelling sounds obvious, but the detail matters. Write the destination room on the side of every box, not just the top, because once boxes are stacked you can only see the sides. A quick note of the contents helps too. We carry boxes straight to the right room as we unload, so a clearly labelled box ends its journey exactly where it belongs rather than in a heap in the hallway.

A simple trick our regulars swear by is colour coding. Assign each room a colour, mark a sheet of paper of that colour on the door of the new home, and put a matching sticker on every box. Even a crew who has never set foot in your house can then unload at speed without asking which room each box belongs to. It costs almost nothing and turns the unpacking days from a scavenger hunt into something genuinely manageable.

Number your boxes too, and keep a simple list of what is in each one. It sounds fussy, but if you are using storage or moving over two days, a numbered inventory means you can find the slow cooker or the spare duvet without opening ten boxes. Mark anything fragile clearly on more than one side, and note which boxes you will want first so we can load them last and unload them first.

sections.h: 4. Plan for Suffolk Access, Parking and Traffic
sections.body:
Our county is beautiful but it does test a removal lorry. Narrow village lanes, a thatched cottage with a low porch, a Bury St Edmunds townhouse with double yellows outside, or a new-build where the nearest the van can get is two doors down. Walk the route at both ends in advance and tell us about anything tight. If you need to suspend parking bays or warn a neighbour, sort it before the day, not on it.

Traffic timing matters too. The A12 and A14 around Ipswich can crawl at rush hour, and the run to the coast around Felixstowe slows on a sunny weekend. We plan our routes to avoid the worst of it, but if your move involves a school-run pinch point or a market-day town centre, give us a heads-up. A few minutes of planning saves a frustrating hour stuck behind a tractor on a single-track lane with the clock running.

It is worth a quick recce of both properties the week before if you can. Note where the lorry can realistically stop, whether there is a height barrier at a car park, and how far the carry from kerb to door actually is. If a neighbour usually parks where the lorry needs to go, a friendly word in advance works wonders. Forewarned, we bring the right vehicle and the right number of crew, and the day runs to time.

sections.h: 5. Keep Children and Pets Safe and Out of the Way
sections.body:
We love a friendly dog and a curious toddler as much as anyone, but moving day is not the day for them to be underfoot. Open doors, heavy furniture and a constant stream of boxes make for a hazardous hallway. Wherever possible, arrange for a friend or relative to look after young children and pets for the day, or set up one quiet room that has already been cleared so they have a safe base while everything else moves around them.

If pets must stay on site, keep them in a closed room with food, water and a familiar blanket, and put a clear note on the door so nobody opens it by mistake. Cats in particular have a habit of bolting when the front door stands open all morning. Tell the crew where they are so we can all keep that door shut. A calm pet and a settled child make the whole day less stressful for everyone, including the movers.

For older children, giving them a small job can work brilliantly; carrying their own bag of toys to the car, or being in charge of the snack box. It keeps them feeling involved rather than uprooted. And when you reach the new home, setting up one familiar corner straight away, a favourite blanket for the dog or a few toys in the child's new room, helps everyone feel that this strange new place is going to be just fine.

sections.h: 6. Look After the Crew, the Paperwork and the Final Walk-Through
sections.body:
A few small courtesies make a genuine difference. Leaving out a kettle, mugs and a packet of biscuits keeps the team fuelled and happy, and a quick chat about any items needing extra care means nothing precious gets treated as just another box. Point out the antiques, the artwork and the boxes marked fragile so we can load them thoughtfully and pad them properly in the van.

Before we drive off, do a proper walk-through of the old place together. Check every cupboard, the loft, the shed, the garden and behind doors, because it is astonishing what gets left behind in the rush. Confirm the inventory, take meter readings, and make sure you have the keys for the new property and a way to hand over the old ones. Then, at the new home, point us to where things go, check everything has arrived safely, and let our crew rebuild the beds so you can collapse into one that night.

That last tip is the one people thank us for most. After a long day, the difference between a made-up bed and a mattress buried under boxes is the difference between a good first night and a miserable one. So make the beds a priority, get the kitchen working enough for breakfast, and leave the rest for tomorrow. The unpacking will still be there in the morning, and a proper rest is the best possible start in your new Suffolk home.

faqs: What should go in my moving day essentials box?

Pack the kettle, mugs, tea and milk, snacks, phone chargers, basic toiletries, a change of clothes, any medication and important documents. Keep it in your own car, not on the lorry, so you can reach everything the moment you arrive.
How can I make moving day faster?

Be fully packed before the crew arrives, label boxes by room on the side, strip the beds and empty the fridge the day before, and clear a path to the door. The more ready you are, the quicker and cheaper the day runs.
Should I be there on moving day?

Yes, ideally. It helps to have someone on hand to point out fragile items, direct boxes to the right rooms and do a final walk-through. If you can arrange childcare or pet care for the day, the whole move runs more smoothly.
What if access is difficult at my property?

Tell us in advance about narrow lanes, low doorways or parking restrictions and we will bring the right vehicle and plan the route. Arranging any parking suspensions or neighbour notice before the day saves valuable time on the morning.
